1: Psalm 24 is a liturgical psalm likely used in a processional for bringing the ark into Jerusalem (cf. 2 Sam 6) or in festival processions to the temple. 'The earth belongs to the LORD and all its fullness; the world and all who live in it' — the comprehensive sovereignty claim. In the ancient Near East, land was understood as divine possession apportioned to peoples and their patron deities (Deut 32:8-9: the Most High apportioned the nations to the sons of God). Ps 24:1 asserts YHWH's ownership of the entire earth, not just Israel's portion. Paul cites this verse in 1 Cor 10:26 to argue that idol-offered food remains under YHWH's ownership.
2: 'For he founded it on the seas and established it above the rivers' — the ancient Israelite cosmology: the earth was conceived as established upon and surrounded by the cosmic waters (tehom, the deep of Gen 1:2; the waters beneath the earth of Exod 20:4). YHWH's act of founding the earth on the seas (yamim) and rivers (neharot) demonstrates his mastery over the primordial waters — the chaotic forces that pre-existed creation. This cosmological claim grounds the ownership claim of v. 1: the one who mastered chaos at creation owns what he created.
3: 'Who may ascend the mountain of the LORD? Who may stand in his holy dwelling?' — the entrance liturgy for temple worship. The 'mountain' (har) is Zion; the 'holy dwelling' (qodesh) is the temple. This entrance question (cf. Ps 15:1, which uses the same form) functioned as a formal challenge at the temple threshold: who has the moral-ritual standing to enter? The form was likely used in actual liturgical practice, with a priestly interrogation at the gates before admission to the sanctuary. Ps 15 and Ps 24 are the two primary entrance liturgy psalms.
4: 'The one with clean hands and a pure heart, who does not set his mind on worthless things or make deceitful promises' — four criteria for temple access: (1) clean hands = ritual and moral purity (the hands were the instruments of action; clean hands = actions that are not morally compromised); (2) pure heart = inner moral integrity; (3) not setting the mind on worthless things = not pursuing idolatry or falsehood; (4) no deceitful oaths = covenant faithfulness in the realm of speech and legal witness. The criteria span from external ritual (hands) to internal orientation (heart) to speech (oaths) — the whole person examined.
5: 'He will receive blessing from the LORD and vindication from the God who saves him' — the promise to those who meet the entrance requirements. 'Vindication' (tsedakah, righteousness/righteous standing) from the God who saves: covenant righteousness as the divine gift that grants the worshiper standing before YHWH. The temple entrance is not just permission to worship; it is the conferral of covenant status — the act by which YHWH declares the worshiper righteous before him.
6: 'Such is the generation of those who seek him, who seek the face of the God of Jacob' — the covenant community defined by its orientation: seeking (dorshav) YHWH and seeking his face (panim). 'Seeking the face' of a king was the formal act of approaching the sovereign in his court for judgment or favor (Esth 4:11; 2 Sam 14:24: Absalom could not 'see the king's face' = had no access to the royal presence). The temple worshiper 'seeks the face of the God of Jacob' — approaches the divine king in his heavenly court, the temple being the earthly access point.
7: 'Lift up your heads, you gates! Rise up, you ancient doors, so the King of glory may enter!' — the antiphonal processional liturgy, likely sung during an actual procession bringing the ark into Jerusalem (2 Sam 6) or in later festival processions to the temple. The gates and doors are personified and commanded to make way for the divine king. 'Ancient doors' (pitche olam, doors of eternity) — the gates of YHWH's royal city, permanent and majestic. The dramatic call-and-response structure (vv. 7-10) suggests actual performance with two groups: the procession and the gatekeepers (or two choirs).
8: 'Who is this King of glory? The LORD, strong and mighty, the LORD, powerful in battle!' — the divine warrior identity. 'King of glory' (melech hakavod) — glory (kavod) as the radiant manifestation of divine power and majesty (Exod 16:10; 24:17; 40:34-35: the kavod filling the tabernacle at its dedication). The response names YHWH as the warrior-king: the ark that is being brought to Zion was the same ark that went before the armies of Israel in battle (Num 10:35-36; 1 Sam 4). Its entrance into Jerusalem is the triumphant procession of the victorious divine general to his throne.
9: 'Lift up your heads, you gates! Rise up, you ancient doors, that the King of glory may enter!' — the deliberate repetition of v. 7, intensifying the processional drama. In ANE processional texts and hymns (such as the Babylonian Akitu festival procession for Marduk), repetition with slight variation was a standard liturgical technique that built anticipation. The gates are addressed twice: once they hesitate or question (v. 8); the second call presses for compliance as the divine king's identity is now declared.
10: 'Who is this King of glory? The LORD of Armies (Yhwh Tseva'ot) — he is the King of glory!' — the climactic identification. Yhwh Tseva'ot (LORD of Armies/Hosts) is one of the most powerful divine titles in the OT: the commander of the armies of heaven (the divine council and angelic hosts) as well as Israel's armies on earth. The title appears frequently in the prophets (Isa 1:9; 6:3) and in the ark narrative (1 Sam 1:3: Eli's sons were at the tent of Yhwh Tseva'ot). The Psalm ends with this double identification: the procession's subject is the cosmic commander-king entering his royal residence.
